text: This video is going to highlight my technique for a fully hand stoned anastomosis in a robotically assisted left hemicolectomy. My concern with these cases has always been that a stapled anastomosis really just didn't lay right And the crotch of the anastomosis always lay over the ligament of trites. That really always bothered me, not just from an aesthetic standpoint, but just technically. I didn't like the way that looked and the way that functioned. So I've always wanted to do this anastomosis hand-sewn end-to-end, truly end-to-end. And really the only way to do that is to do it fully hand sewn with no staple line. And obviously the robot really is pretty much the only way to do that.

The surgeon performs a standard left hemicolectomy on a patient with a tumor near the splenic flexure. After stapling both ends of the resected colon and setting the specimen aside, the surgeon sews the staple lines together, preparing for a hand-sewn two-layered anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the hand-sewn anastomosis using a double-ended V-lock suture, creating an enterotomy on both sides. The first suture is placed through the loop at the corner, aligning the anastomosis. The surgeon holds the staple lines with a third hand, ensuring full thickness stitches across the proximal and distal ends. The technique involves stitching from outside in and inside out, emphasizing full thickness at each step.

The surgeon uses a double-ended stitch to line up the posterior line of the anastomosis, holding the stitch with a third arm for precise placement. A second anterior layer stitch is added early on, ensuring even placement on both proximal and distal ends. The surgeon carefully runs the stitch back to confirm the correct needle is used, achieving full thickness across the defect.

The surgeon demonstrates a hand-sewn anastomosis technique, focusing on achieving full thickness closure of the two lumens while minimizing mucosal protrusion. The anterior Lembert stitch is applied superiorly, running straight down to the previous stitch. Care is taken to avoid creating a purse-string effect by ensuring stitches are taut but not overly tight, maintaining lumen patency. The third hand is used to align the lumens evenly across the proximal and distal ends.

The surgeon opens the lumen and performs hand-sewn suturing using the Lembert technique, carefully manipulating the colon edges with needle drivers. The Lembert sutures are placed to bring the tissue together smoothly, avoiding excessive gripping of the bowel wall. The final stitches are simple, with the surgeon lifting the distal end to avoid grabbing the back wall, ensuring secure closure.

The surgeon completes the anterior second layer of the anastomosis by taking seromuscular bites, aligning it with the posterior first layer at the staple line. The staple lines help maintain alignment and prevent skipping spots, ensuring a precise and consistent closure.
